basmala: In the Name of God, the Infinitely Merciful, the Most Merciful.
intro: An-Nās — “Mankind” — is the last sūra of the Quran, and the second of the Muʿawwidhatān (المُعَوِّذَتَان), the “two protectresses”. If al-Falaq taught one to take refuge with the Lord of every emergence, an-Nās descends into a still more intimate place: the breast, the ṣadr (صَدْر), where the soul speaks of itself. Tustarī gives the key from the start: “All that is other than God is a whispering. When the heart of a man is with God, it speaks from God; if it is with another than Him, it speaks from that other.” And further: “The knowledge of the lower self (nafs, نَفْس) is more hidden than the knowledge of the Enemy; and the knowledge of the Enemy is more apparent than the knowledge of the world.” The sūra is therefore not only a magical protection against outward demons: it is the training of a vigilance — that which distinguishes, in the stream of thoughts, the ilhām (إِلْهَام), true inspiration, from the waswās (وَسْوَاس), the whisper wearing the face of the self. Tustarī concludes: “You must have sincerity (ikhlāṣ, إِخْلَاص) to be preserved from whispering. Beware of scheming and self-management (tadbīr, تَدْبِير): it is a sickness of the lower soul.”

versets.trad: Say: “I seek refuge with the Lord of mankind,
versets.commentaire: Three Names will be pronounced in the opening — Rabb, Malik, Ilāh. For Qushayrī, it is an ascent that answers three ways in which the creature depends on God: He is the Lord who brings into being (Rabb), the King who orders every thing (Malik), the sole God toward whom worship goes (Ilāh). Three ways of stating, from three angles, the same absolute Lordship — and therefore the servant's total dependence. Aʿūdhu, “I take refuge”, is the beginning of acknowledging this dependence, of letting go the illusion of being one's own Lord.

mots.def: to say, to utter, to pronounce; in the imperative: “say”, “proclaim”.
to seek protection, to take refuge with someone so as to be guarded from a danger or an evil.
rabb: the Lord, the Master; the one who owns a thing, nourishes it, raises it and brings it to its full growth.
mots.gram: preposition bi- + noun in the genitive

mots.def: mankind, people, humanity; from a root evoking familiarity, sociability, life in company.

versets.commentaire: Malik (مَلِك), the King: He from whom every true authority derives. Qushayrī notes simply — “the Master of them all”. Tustarī, when he names the King, looks at the inner kingdom: within man lies a realm where forces vie for the attention — the intellect, the passion, the imagination, the memories. To acknowledge that God alone is its King is to cease letting those forces reign. It is to make silence within, that one voice may rule — the One whom one calls when one says aʿūdhu.

mots.def: the king, the sovereign; the one who holds authority and the power to command and to govern.
mankind, people, humanity.

versets.commentaire: Ilāh (إِلَٰه), the God — the sole One toward whom worship goes. The ascent of the three Names is complete: He brings into being (Rabb), He governs (Malik), He alone is worshipped (Ilāh). According to Sufi commentators, the three answer to three planes of the human being: Rabb meets the nafs (the animated soul that asks for what it needs to live); Malik meets the qalb (the heart that needs an inner order); Ilāh meets the sirr (the inmost secret whose proper food is worship itself). To seek refuge before these three Names joined is to ask that the whole person, from the flesh to the secret, be turned back to God alone.

mots.def: the deity, that which is worshipped; the being toward whom worship and devotion turn.
mankind, people, humanity.

versets.trad: from the harm of the furtive whisperer,
versets.commentaire: al-waswās (الوَسْوَاس), the whisper; al-khannās (الخَنَّاس), the one who withdraws. Qushayrī explains: “al-Khannās is the one who hides and slips away before the remembrance of God — it is one of the attributes of Satan.” The whisper does not remain before the clear face of dhikr: it retreats when the soul remembers, and returns when it forgets. That is why the sūra is not only a request for refuge — it is itself dhikr: to pronounce it is to make recede what it denounces. Tustarī: “You must have sincerity (ikhlāṣ) to be preserved from whispering. Beware of scheming and self-management (tadbīr, تَدْبِير) — it is a sickness of the soul.”

mots.def: preposition: from, out of; here, the idea of protection against.
evil, harm; that which is bad or harmful — the opposite of good (khayr).
the insidious murmur, the suggestion repeated in a low voice; by extension, the one who utters it.
khannās: intensive form — the one who slips away and hides; for Qushayrī, “the one who withdraws before the remembrance of God”.

versets.trad: who whispers doubt into the breasts of mankind —
versets.commentaire: The verse descends into the intimate place — ṣudūr (صُدُور), the breast, the inmost where a word resounds before any sound is uttered. Tustarī writes here the phrase that lights the whole sūra: “All that is other than God is a whisper. When the heart of a man is with God, it speaks from God; if it is with another than Him, it speaks from that other.” And he makes a decisive distinction: “The place of the whisper, with regard to the servant, is that of the soul that commands to evil (al-nafs al-ammāra bi-l-sūʾ, النَّفْس الأَمَّارَة بِالسُّوء) when it suggests something to the nature. The whisper of the Enemy is in the breasts, the whisper of the soul is in the heart — for God has said: We know what his soul whispers to him, and We are nearer to him than his jugular vein (50:16). The knowledge of the soul is more hidden than the knowledge of the Enemy.” That is to say: it is harder still to recognise the voice that speaks within us in our own name than the one that comes from without. Qushayrī adds, for discernment: “By knowledge one distinguishes between inspiration (ilhām, إِلْهَام), sound thoughts, and whisperings (waswās).”

mots.def: relative pronoun, masculine singular: who, which.
yuwaswisu: to murmur an insidious suggestion, to whisper a low voice repeatedly — the word imitates the thing by alliteration.
preposition: in, inside, into.
ṣadr: the breast, the seat of the heart and of the inmost self; in the plural, ṣudūr, the inner selves.
mankind, people, humanity.

versets.trad: whether of jinn or of mankind.”
versets.commentaire: The enemy is of two orders — the jinn (the unseen, the subtle) and mankind (the visible, the rumour of others). But in the Sufi reading, the opposition shifts: there is an Enemy from without and an Enemy from within, and the second is harder to know. Tustarī gives the strategy: “The way to capture the enemy is to know him. When you know him, you have captured him; but if you do not know that he is the enemy, then he has captured you.” To know one's own lower soul — its way of putting on the face of piety, of judgment, of kindness — is a life's work. And the whole Quran ends upon this word, an-nās, “mankind”, repeated four times in the sūra: the Book closes upon man — the one who seeks himself, and who will only find himself by handing over to God all that in him takes itself for God.

mots.def: preposition min: from, among, being part of.
the jinn, the beings of the hidden world; from a root evoking what is veiled, hidden from sight.
coordinating conjunction: and; joins two terms or two clauses.
mankind, people, humanity.
